concentration.headline: Highly distributed urban network
concentration.body: No single city overwhelms Canada's urban landscape. Toronto holds 14.2% of tracked urban population across 485 cities — characteristic of federal or historically polycentric countries with multiple strong economic centers.
concentration.comparison: Similar to: United States, Germany, Switzerland

gravity.headline: Dense gravitational cluster — Toronto & Mississauga
gravity.body: The strongest gravitational pull exists between Toronto and Mississauga (27 km apart, combined population 6,365,617). Cities form a tight gravitational cluster with top pairs averaging 47 km apart. This dense sub-regional network often functions as a single megalopolitan economy.

narration.synthesis: Canada presents a classic polycentric structure: no primate city, with Toronto holding just 14.2% of tracked urban population. Gravity is strongest within a tight sub-regional cluster (Toronto → Mississauga, 47 km average separation) rather than radiating from a single capital — typical of federal economies with distributed industrial heritage.
